title: Equipment Selection and Types
narrationScript: Walk into any shop that does magnetic particle work and you will see equipment ranging from a two thousand pound bench fixture the size of a lathe to a yoke you can carry in one hand. None of it is interchangeable. Picking the right machine is a technical decision, and it starts with five questions. First, what magnetizing current does the job need. Alternating current rides the surface because of skin effect and gives particles excellent mobility, so it is the choice for surface flaws. Half wave rectified current pulses, which reaches slightly below the surface and keeps dry powder dancing, the classic combination for structural welds. Full wave rectified current penetrates deepest and suits bench inspection of machined parts. Second, where is the test and what is its nature. A pipeline weld on a right of way needs something you can carry. Ten thousand fasteners a day need a fixed station with a recirculating bath. Third, what is the test material. Alloy, heat treat condition, and surface finish drive current type, particle choice, and whether the residual method is even possible. High retentivity steels allow residual inspection. Soft steels demand the continuous method. Fourth, what is the purpose of the test. A critical aerospace overhaul justifies fluorescent wet method in a darkened booth, while a structural weld may only need visible dry powder and a yoke. And fifth, how much area must be inspected. Full coverage of a complex part means multiple shots in multiple directions, which favors equipment that can do both circular and longitudinal magnetization without re fixturing. Now the hardware. Stationary equipment means the wet horizontal bench unit. The part clamps between a headstock and tailstock for a head shot, which passes current through the part for circular magnetization, or a coil rides the rails for longitudinal magnetization. A central conductor threads through hollow parts to put a circular field on inside surfaces. Bench units deliver high amperage, commonly thousands of amperes, recirculate a fluorescent bath, carry a built in ammeter and shot timer, and often include demagnetization capability. They are the production and overhaul standard. Mobile equipment is the middle ground. A power pack on wheels delivers high amperage, often a few thousand amperes, of alternating or half wave current through cables. You wrap the cable around a large casting for a coil shot, or connect prods or clamps for circular work. Mobile units go to the part when the part cannot come to the bench, think large weldments, castings, and shafts in a repair bay. Portable equipment goes anywhere a technician can climb. The electromagnetic yoke is the everyday tool, switchable between alternating and direct current, magnetizing the area between its poles. Its standard performance check is lifting power, typically a ten pound dead weight lift for alternating current, and about forty pounds for direct current yokes, at the pole spacing being used. Prods carry current into the part through two hand held electrodes, creating a circular field around the current path. Typical procedure values run about ninety to one hundred twenty five amperes per inch of prod spacing, with spacing commonly six to eight inches. Prods are powerful and fast on heavy weldments, but the contact points can arc and leave burns, which is why many aerospace and finished surface specifications prohibit them. Permanent magnet yokes exist for areas where no power or no arcing of any kind is allowed, at the cost of weaker, fixed strength fields. Multidirectional units energize two or three circuits in rapid alternation, putting fields in more than one direction during the same shot so one operation finds flaws in any orientation. The balance between the outputs is critical, and it must be proven with shim type flaw indicators in each direction before the technique is trusted. Automatic equipment takes the human out of the loop for high volume parts, handling the conveyance, the shots, the bath, and increasingly the reading, with cameras and reject gates. Automation makes setup verification more important, not less, because a bad setup rejects or passes parts by the thousand. Finally, demagnetization equipment, the pass through alternating current coil at the end of the bench, step down units, and reversing direct current systems built into bench machines. We covered how they work in the last lesson. Here, just remember they are part of the equipment plan, because a shop that magnetizes parts all day needs an engineered way to un-magnetize them too. Match the machine to the current, the location, the material, the purpose, and the coverage, and the rest of the inspection falls into place.
imagePrompt: A wide view of an NDT shop showing a long wet horizontal magnetic particle bench unit with a steel shaft clamped between its heads, while a technician nearby carries a compact yellow magnetic yoke toward a welded steel frame.

sections.heading: Five Questions Before You Pick a Machine
sections.body:
Equipment selection is a technical decision driven by the job, not by what happens to be plugged in. Practices such as ASTM E709 and E1444 frame the same five considerations the CP-105 outline lists:

- **Type of magnetizing current (9.1.1).** AC for surface flaws and particle mobility; HWDC for near-surface capability with dry powder; FWDC for the deepest penetration on bench units.
- **Location and nature of the test (9.1.2).** Shop or field? One weld or a production stream? Power available, or battery and engine-driven only?
- **Test materials (9.1.3).** Alloy, heat-treat condition, and finish set the current, the particles, and whether the residual method is even permitted — high-retentivity steels allow it; soft steels demand the continuous method.
- **Purpose of the test (9.1.4).** Critical rotating aerospace hardware justifies fluorescent wet method in a darkened booth; a structural fillet weld may need only visible dry powder and a yoke.
- **Area inspected (9.1.5).** Full coverage requires two roughly perpendicular field directions everywhere — favoring equipment that can deliver circular and longitudinal magnetization without re-fixturing.

sections.caption: Selection considerations mapped to typical equipment outcomes.
rows: Surface only, or near-surface too? How thick?
AC yoke for surface cracks; HWDC + dry powder for weld subsurface; FWDC bench for machined parts
Pipeline weld → portable yoke; 5,000 parts/day → stationary or automatic
Hardened steel may permit residual method; mild steel → continuous
Aerospace overhaul → fluorescent wet bench; structural weld → visible dry powder
Local welds or 100% of a complex part?
Full coverage → bench with head shot + coil, or multidirectional unit

sections.heading: Stationary Equipment: The Wet Horizontal Bench
sections.body:
The **wet horizontal bench unit** is the production standard. The part clamps between a **headstock and tailstock** for a **head shot** — current passes through the part, creating a *circular* field that finds longitudinal flaws. A **coil** mounted on the rails slides over the part for *longitudinal* magnetization that finds transverse flaws. A **central conductor** bar threads through hollow parts to put a circular field on inside diameters without passing current through the part itself.

Bench units deliver high amperage — commonly thousands of amperes FWDC or AC — with an ammeter to verify each shot, a shot timer, and a recirculating fluorescent bath with agitation to keep particles in suspension. Many include built-in demagnetization (reversing step-down DC or an AC decay cycle). With a darkened curtain or booth and a UV-A lamp, one station covers both field directions, the bath, and demag — which is exactly why machined production and overhaul parts go to the bench.

sections.caption: A wet horizontal bench unit: head shot for circular magnetization, movable coil for longitudinal, recirculating bath, and UV-A lamp at the station.

sections.heading: Mobile and Portable Equipment
sections.body:
**Mobile units (9.3)** are wheeled power packs delivering high amperage — often a few thousand amperes of AC or HWDC — through heavy cables. Wrap the cable around a large casting for a coil shot, or connect **prods or contact clamps** for circular magnetization. Mobile equipment brings bench-class amperage to parts that cannot come to the bench: large weldments, housings, and shafts in a repair bay.

**Portable equipment (9.4)** goes wherever the technician climbs:

- **Electromagnetic yokes** — AC/DC switchable, magnetizing the area between the poles. Performance is proven by a **lifting-power check: about 10 lb (AC) or about 40 lb (DC)** dead weight at the pole spacing used, per typical procedure requirements.
- **Prods** — two hand-held electrodes pass current through the part, creating a circular field around the current path. Typical procedure values run **90–125 A per inch of prod spacing**, with spacing commonly **6–8 in.** Fast and powerful on heavy weldments — but see the safety callout.
- **Permanent-magnet yokes** — for no-power or no-arc-risk areas; weaker, fixed-strength fields are the tradeoff.

sections.title: Prods: arc strikes and broken contact
sections.body: Prod tips can **arc** where they meet the part — a momentary arc strike creates a hard, cracked martensitic spot that can be worse than the flaw you were hunting. Keep tips clean, seat both prods firmly **before** energizing, and **never break contact while current flows**. Many aerospace and finished-surface specifications **prohibit prods outright**; your written procedure decides. The same discipline applies at bench head shots: clamp solidly, energize, then release only after the shot ends.

sections.caption: Portable magnetization: a yoke puts a longitudinal field between its poles, while prods drive a circular field around the current path between their tips.

sections.heading: Multidirectional, Automatic, and Demag Equipment
sections.body:
**Multidirectional units (9.5)** energize two or three magnetizing circuits in rapid alternation — for example a head shot plus a coil — so fields in more than one direction act during the same shot, and one operation reveals flaws of any orientation. The hazard is **imbalance**: if one circuit dominates, the weaker direction quietly disappears. Balance must be **proven with artificial-flaw shims (QQIs) oriented for each field direction** before the technique is trusted, and re-proven when the setup changes.

**Automatic equipment (9.6)** conveys parts through magnetization, bath application, and increasingly automated reading — UV cameras, image processing, reject gates — for high-volume production such as fasteners and bearing components. Automation raises the stakes on setup: a bad setup passes or rejects parts by the thousand, so system checks with known-defect reference parts are the backbone of the operation.

**Demagnetization equipment (9.7)** completes the station plan: pass-through AC coils at the end of the bench line, AC step-down units, and reversing step-down DC built into bench machines. A shop that magnetizes parts all day needs an engineered way to un-magnetize them — and to verify it with a field indicator.

figures.title: Portable tools: yoke field versus prod field

checkQuestions.question: A girth weld on a pipeline right-of-way must be inspected for surface cracks; only a small generator is available. The most appropriate equipment is:
options: A wet horizontal bench unit
An AC/DC electromagnetic yoke with dry or aerosol particles
An automatic conveyor system
A multidirectional bench with recirculating bath
checkQuestions.explanation: Location and nature of the test drive selection: a portable yoke needs minimal power, travels to the weld, and AC excels at the surface cracks in question — bench, automatic, and multidirectional systems are fixed-shop equipment.

checkQuestions.question: For near-surface flaw capability with dry powder on structural welds in the field, the classic current and equipment pairing is:
options: FWDC from a bench unit
AC from a permanent-magnet yoke
HWDC from a mobile power pack or portable unit with prods
Pure AC through a central conductor
checkQuestions.explanation: Half-wave DC pulsates — reaching slightly subsurface while keeping dry powder mobile — and mobile/prod equipment delivers it to field weldments; permanent magnets supply no current at all.

checkQuestions.question: Using prods at a 6-inch spacing, a typical procedure amperage would be about:
checkQuestions.explanation: Typical procedure values run about 90–125 A per inch of prod spacing, so 6 in. × 90–125 A/in. gives roughly 540–750 A — always confirmed against your written procedure.

checkQuestions.question: Before relying on a multidirectional unit, the balance between its field directions must be verified using:
options: The built-in ammeter alone
A lifting-power test with a 10-lb weight
Artificial-flaw shims (QQIs) oriented for each field direction
A pocket field indicator held at the part's end
checkQuestions.explanation: QQI shims placed for each direction prove that every field in the multidirectional cycle produces adequate indications; an ammeter only proves current, and lifting tests apply to yokes.

checkQuestions.question: A hollow cylindrical part needs its inside-diameter surface inspected for longitudinal flaws on a bench unit. The correct technique is:
options: A head shot through the part walls
A central conductor through the bore, carrying the current
A coil shot over the outside
A permanent-magnet yoke inside the bore
checkQuestions.explanation: A central conductor places a circular field on the ID (and OD) without passing current through the part — a head shot concentrates on the OD, and a coil gives longitudinal, not circular, magnetization.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §9.1 (equipment selection considerations 9.1.1–9.1.5)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §9.2–9.7 (stationary, mobile, portable, multidirectional, automatic, demagnetization equipment)
Practices such as ASTM E709, ASTM E1444, and ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 (equipment, yoke lifting checks, prod practice — general practice)
